cities.name: Nairobi
cities.typology_description: Flash flooding driven by inadequate stormwater infrastructure and systematic encroachment of 30m riparian reserves. Political economy: land value of riparian plots creates enforcement resistance. The enforcement gap is structural and politically sustained.

cities.name: Kisumu
cities.typology_label: Lake Victoria basin rise + riverine overflow
cities.typology_description: Lake Victoria water levels rose ~1.5m between 2019-2021, inundating lakeside settlements. 2020 floods displaced over 100,000 people in Nyanza region. Early warning must key to lake gauge levels, not rainfall alone — a fundamentally different monitoring architecture than Nairobi.
cities.key_policy_gap: Lake Victoria buffer zone enforcement + Nyando basin management
cities.season_peak: March-May, elevated risk during La Nina years (lake level rise)

cities.name: Mombasa
cities.typology_label: Coastal storm surge + urban drainage + Indian Ocean exposure
cities.typology_description: Compound flood risk: Indian Ocean storm surge, inadequate drainage in low-lying areas, and tidal creek encroachment. IPCC projects 0.3-0.6m sea level rise by 2100, permanently inundating parts of Mombasa Island. Mangrove destruction has removed the natural surge buffer. Requires coastal engineering responses distinct from upcountry approaches.
cities.implementing_body: Mombasa County Government + Kenya Ports Authority + NEMA
cities.season_peak: April-June, compounded by April-May Indian Ocean swells

cities.name: Nakuru
cities.typology_label: Endorheic lake level rise — no natural outlet
cities.typology_description: Lake Nakuru rose approximately 10 metres between 2013-2023, inundating the KWS buffer zone and displacing lakeside communities. As an endorheic basin with no river outlet, water only leaves through evaporation. Conventional flood management is inapplicable. The response must centre on managed retreat and permanent buffer zone establishment — a policy that requires political will to deliver.
cities.implementing_body: Nakuru County Government + Kenya Wildlife Service + WRMA
cities.key_policy_gap: Endorheic basin management framework + managed retreat policy
cities.season_peak: Cumulative lake level rise — not seasonal. La Nina years are highest risk.

cities.name: Garissa
cities.typology_label: Tana River riverine flooding — pastoral communities, seasonal displacement
cities.typology_description: The Tana River — fed by Mount Kenya and Aberdare rainfall — floods Garissa and surrounding pastoral areas seasonally, with major events in 2020 and 2023. Flood-affected communities are often the same communities dependent on the Tana for livestock water. Last-mile early warning to dispersed pastoral settlements is the critical unsolved gap. Tana Integrated Water Resources Management Project remains incomplete after 15 years.
cities.implementing_body: Garissa County Government + TANA Water Works + NDOC
cities.key_policy_gap: Tana River basin management + last-mile SMS early warning for pastoral communities
cities.season_peak: Driven by upstream Mt Kenya/Aberdare rainfall — not local. Peak: April-May, November.
